nativeRange.habitat: Emergent aquatic plant of ponds, lakes and slow-moving streams across the Mississippi River basin, from central Texas and the Florida Panhandle north to southern Illinois.

summary: A sturdy, broad-leaved rosette plant that spreads via runners to form a grassy background stand; the larger, taller cousin of Sagittaria subulata and a forgiving choice for medium to large planted tanks.
careNotes: Sagittaria platyphylla is widely sold under the trade names 'Broadleaf Chilensis', 'Broadleaf/Delta Arrowhead' and 'Giant Sagittaria'. It is meaningfully larger than Sagittaria subulata (fw-plant-047) — typical submerged growth in aquaria reaches roughly 25–50 cm, which generally places it as a background plant rather than a foreground/midground carpet, although under very strong light it stays more compact and can be used as a tall midground accent. Light demand is on the moderate end (Modern Aquarium calls for 'moderate–strong' light; hobbyist guides agree moderate full-spectrum LED light at 6,500–7,500 K is the steady-growth target). CO2 is generally not required but is beneficial in high-energy setups — supplementation produces denser, more compact leaves and faster runners. The species is a fairly heavy root feeder; loosely packed, nutrient-rich planted-tank soil is ideal but it also grows well in fine gravel or sand when supplemented with root tabs. Iron supplementation is commonly recommended if leaves yellow or pale. Temperature is broadly tolerated in the 22–28 °C range. The plant is amphibious — emergent leaves develop ovate to elliptical blades on tall petioles (up to ~150 cm in the wild, where it grows as a marsh plant with arrow-shaped emergent foliage), so open-top tanks or paludariums can be allowed to grow it emersed for the characteristic arrowhead leaves. Propagation in aquaria is essentially all by stolons (runners); a healthy mother plant will continuously send out new rosettes that can be thinned or replanted to keep the layout tidy. Native to the eastern United States across the Mississippi River basin (central Texas and the Florida Panhandle north to southern Illinois) — despite the 'Chilensis' trade name, it is NOT a South American species. IMPORTANT: this species is a declared noxious weed in Australia and has invaded the Yangtze River basin in China; aquarists in those jurisdictions should never release plant material into local waterways and should check local legality before purchase.
breedingNotes: Propagates very readily by stolons (runners) from the base of the mother plant; each runner produces a new rosette that can be separated and replanted once it has its own roots. The wild plant also reproduces by seed (white flowers in racemes) and develops underground corms, but neither is a meaningful propagation route in aquaria.

primary.name: Modern Aquarium
primary.notes: Modern Aquarium product page. Lists temperature 72–82 °F (~22–28 °C), moderate–strong light, CO2 not required, easy care level, moderate-to-fast growth, foreground–midground placement, runner propagation, nutrient-rich substrate preferred with root tabs in sand/gravel, iron supplementation if leaves pale. Native range stated as 'North America and Southeast Asia' — partly incorrect; Wikipedia confirms the species is US-native and only invasive in Asia/Australia, so the careNotes record the corrected native range.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Sagittaria platyphylla
additional.notes: Taxonomic and biogeographic cross-check. Confirms family Alismataceae, order Alismatales, native range central Texas–Florida Panhandle north to southern Illinois (Mississippi basin), amphibious growth with emergent ovate-elliptical blades up to 17 cm and overall plant up to 150 cm emergent in the wild, propagation by stolons/seeds/corms, noxious-weed status in Australia and invasive in China's Yangtze basin. Common names: delta arrowhead, broad-leaf arrowhead, delta duck-potato.

additional.name: Splashy Fish — Sagittaria Care Guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked submerged aquarium height (~25–50 cm / 10–20 in), tank size guidance (20+ US gallons → 75 L floor), moderate light recommendation (6,500–7,500 K LED, 8–10 h photoperiod), runner propagation, moderate growth rate, CO2 optional. Resolves the height/placement question — Modern Aquarium's foreground/midground claim disagreed with the user-supplied note that this is a background plant; cross-source consensus on 25–50 cm submerged plus the comparison to S. subulata (10–30 cm) confirms background placement is correct.

propagation: seed (wild only, not practical in aquaria)
